extra.reasoning: The property requires that rec_drug_a and rec_drug_b are never jointly true. rec_drug_b is set true by rule rec_b_from_trigger (priority 50) whenever qt_prolongation holds. rec_drug_a is set true by rule rec_a_from_conjunction (priority 50) whenever qt_prolongation AND heart_failure AND anticoagulated AND low_ejection_fraction all hold. The trigger condition for rec_drug_a is a strict superset of (and includes) qt_prolongation, the trigger for rec_drug_b. Therefore any input satisfying the rec_drug_a conjunction necessarily also satisfies the rec_drug_b trigger. Priorities do not prevent this because priority/conflict resolution operates per-output (rec_drug_a vs rec_drug_b are different outputs), and there are no rules that ever set rec_drug_a or rec_drug_b to false (the default only applies when an output is left unset). With qt_prolongation=true, heart_failure=true, anticoagulated=true, low_ejection_fraction=true (other inputs false), rec_drug_b fires (qt_prolongation true) and rec_drug_a fires (all four conjuncts true), making both jointly true. This violates the mutual exclusion property.
